full_response:
Let  

• R = r0 r1 … r​n-1 (the characters written clockwise on the ring, indices mod n)  
• s = s0 s1 … s​m-1 (the word Balbo is looking for, m ≤ n)

The two different symbols that appear on the first ring and in s will be
denoted by α and β (|Σ|>2 guarantees that any other symbols can be ignored).

Goal Report every 0 ≤ i < n such that for every 0 ≤ j < m  

             r(i+j) mod n = s​j .



--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  Reducing the cyclic problem to an ordinary substring problem
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Append the first m–1 characters of the ring to itself  

             T = R ◦ R[0…m–2]                      // length N = n+m−1

Every cyclic length-m fragment of the ring now appears **exactly once**
as an ordinary length-m substring of T starting somewhere in the first n
positions.  Consequently

s occurs on the ring starting at position i
⇔   s equals T[i … i+m−1] (0 ≤ i < n).                          (1)



--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  Converting the two–letter alphabet to numbers
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Map the two symbols to ±1:

     f(α)=+1 , f(β)=–1                               (2)

(All α/β choices work as long as they are consistent.)

Define two numeric arrays

     A[k] = f(T[k])                    for 0 ≤ k < N (=n+m−1)  
     B[k] = f(s[m−1−k])                for 0 ≤ k < m   (s reversed) (3)



--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  Why a single convolution detects perfect matches
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Consider a shift i (0≤i<n).  
Correlation value

   C[i] =  Σ_{j=0}^{m−1}  A[i+j] · B[m−1−j]
        = Σ_{j=0}^{m−1} f(T[i+j]) · f(s[j])            (by (3)) (4)

• If T[i+j] = s​j then the product is (+1)(+1)=1 or (–1)(–1)=1.  
• If T[i+j] ≠ s​j exactly one of the pair is –1, so the product is –1.

Let k be the number of mismatches in this window.  Then

                            C[i] = (+1)(m–k) + (–1)k = m – 2k.    (5)

Hence

             C[i] = m     ⇔    k = 0    ⇔    perfect match.       (6)



--------------------------------------------------------------------
4.  Computing all C[i] with one FFT
--------------------------------------------------------------------
The correlation in (4) is the (m−1)-shift of the convolution of A and B.
Let L be the next power of two ≥ N+m.  Pad A and B with zeros to length L.

         FA = FFT(A),  FB = FFT(B)                  // O(L log L)  
         FC[k] = FA[k]·FB[k]  (element-wise product)  
         C = IFFT(FC).                                               (7)

After rounding to the nearest integer (to kill numerical noise) we have
C[m−1 … m−1+n−1] = C[m−1 … m+n−2] equal to the values of (4).



--------------------------------------------------------------------
5.  Pseudocode
--------------------------------------------------------------------
FFT-Match-Ring(R[0…n−1], s[0…m−1])  
    N ← n+m−1  
    // build T
    for k ← 0 … N−1
        T[k] ← R[ k mod n ]
    // numeric arrays
    for k ← 0 … N−1       A[k] ← (T[k]==α) ? +1 : −1
    for k ← 0 … m−1       B[k] ← (s[m−1−k]==α) ? +1 : −1
    choose L = power-of-two ≥ N+m
    pad A,B to length L with zeros
    FA ← FFT(A) ; FB ← FFT(B)
    for k ←0 … L−1        FC[k] ← FA[k] · FB[k]
    C ← IFFT(FC)
    ans ← ∅
    for i ← 0 … n−1
        if round( C[i+m−1] ) == m
              output i        // s starts at ring position i
    return ans



--------------------------------------------------------------------
6.  Correctness proof
--------------------------------------------------------------------
We prove that the algorithm outputs exactly those i that satisfy (1).

(⇒)  Suppose the algorithm outputs i.  
      Then round(C[i+m−1]) = m, so by (6) k = 0 mismatches; therefore
      T[i … i+m−1] = s, and (1) holds.

(⇐)  Conversely, assume (1) holds for some i.  
      All m characters match, so k = 0.  By (5) C[i] = m, hence
      after rounding C[i+m−1] = m, so the algorithm outputs i.

Thus the set of reported positions is exactly the set of occurrences. ∎



--------------------------------------------------------------------
7.  Complexity analysis
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Building the arrays:    Θ(n+m)  
One forward FFT of length L: O(L log L)  
One inverse  FFT of length L: O(L log L)  
Element-wise multiply:  Θ(L)

With L = Θ(n+m), total running time  

        T(n,m) = Θ((n+m) log(n+m)).  

All auxiliary arrays have length Θ(n+m), so the algorithm uses
Θ(n+m) space.



\boxed{\text{All occurrences of }s\text{ in the ring can be found in }
O((n+m)\log(n+m))\text{ time and }O(n+m)\text{ space by one FFT-based convolution
as described above.}}

Confidence: 0.77
